data.title: Stitch Fix: A Compelling 'Buy' As Order Values Rise (Upgrade)
data.description:
With the recent choppiness in the stock market, we certainly have to be more careful with which stocks we invest in, as there is no longer a rising tide to lift all boats. But that said, we also shouldn't ignore small-cap names that present unique value opportunities just because the market is taking a risk-off stance.

Stitch Fix (SFIX), the e-commerce retailer once best known for sending its customers a "Fix" of five items that they selected from, is looking like an increasingly attractive bet. Since the start of the year, Stitch Fix has lost more than a third of its value. But at the same time, the company has delivered improving revenue trends, stable margins, and can justify its valuation fully from its bottom-line performance. The question for investors now is: Will Stitch Fix continue falling as investors punish small caps, or is the selloff now overdone?

I last wrote a "Neutral" article on Stitch Fix in December, when the stock was trading at $5 per share. Since then, two major factors have shifted the tide in Stitch Fix's favor. The first and most obvious is the fact that Stitch Fix's valuation has crumbled, making its valuation multiples more enticing. At the same time, amid a tough macroeconomy with weak consumer spending, Stitch Fix may actually be pulling ahead and gaining market share, as evidenced by its accelerating sales in a tough holiday quarter. With all of these indicators tilting positive, I'm turning cautiously optimistic on this stock and upgrading it to a "Buy."

To me, I now view more positives than negatives in this stock. The core tailwinds that drive the bull case for this company are:

  • Clear shift toward higher-spending customers. Yes, it's true that Stitch Fix has consistently lost active clients over the past few years. At the same time, the company is converging on a more loyal, higher-spending customer base, which isn't necessarily a bad thing, as the company can pull back on advertising spend and focus on serving these repeat customers.
  • Stylists provide Stitch Fix's differentiating factor. There are countless e-commerce sites in the market, and needless to say, it's difficult to stand out, especially in a tough macro. Stitch Fix's stylist-driven personalization provides the company's unique factor, which appeals to customers who want a curated look.
  • Stabilizing margins. Despite headwinds from tariffs, Stitch Fix has managed to maintain stable gross margins in the mid-40%s, with the company also consistently generating positive adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow. The company is committed to opex discipline, with the company noting that it eliminated $120 million of annual SG&A spending in FY25.
  • Strong balance sheet. Unlike what one might expect from a smaller retailer with a choppy history for execution, Stitch Fix has over $0.2 billion in cash completely unencumbered of debt, on top of ongoing cash flow - giving the company plenty of financial flexibility.

Valuation also provides a compelling impetus to buy this stock. At current share prices near $3, Stitch Fix trades at a market cap of $458.0 million. Netting off the $240.5 million of cash against zero debt on Stitch Fix's most recent balance sheet yields an enterprise value of $217.5 million.

For the remainder of FY26 (the fiscal year ending in July 2026), Stitch Fix has guided to $1.33-$1.35 billion in revenue (5-6% full-year revenue growth), and 3.2%-3.7% adjusted EBITDA margin, which translates to $42-$50 million in adjusted EBITDA.

This positions Stitch Fix's valuation at 4.7x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA. With the company returning to near-double-digit revenue growth and potentially on the cusp of positive margin inflection, we think this is an overly pessimistic multiple.

I wouldn't bet the proverbial farm on a small-cap name like Stitch Fix given the choppy execution history and macro risks, but in my view, this stock is now worth a small position.

Q2 download

Let's now go through Stitch Fix's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The fiscal Q2 (January quarter) earnings summary is shown below:

Stitch Fix's revenue grew 9% y/y to $341.3 million, handily beating Wall Street's expectations of $332.8 million (+7% y/y) by a two-point margin. Growth also accelerated two points versus 7% y/y growth in Q1, which is closer to what Wall Street had been expecting.

Stitch Fix seems to have found the formula that works for it: fewer, but higher-spending customers. In Q2, the company still shed 19k active customers sequentially versus Q1 (defined as somebody who has made a purchase within the trailing twelve months). The active customer base fell 3.5% y/y.

At the same time, revenue per active client is rising sharply: up 3.2% sequentially and 7.4% y/y to $577. This is more than offsetting client losses to drive high-single-digit revenue growth.

Moreover, Stitch Fix notes that the broader apparel landscape saw a slight y/y decline per third-party data. Based on this, Stitch Fix is actually gaining market share, which is not something its low-single-digit adjusted EBITDA multiples would suggest. Per CEO Matt Baer's remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

We also continued to gain market share and significantly outperform the broader U.S. apparel and accessories market during the quarter, highlighting the strength of our value proposition. Our 9.4% year-over-year revenue growth in Q2 contrasts with the 0.5% contraction the total U.S. apparel, footwear and accessories market sustained in the same period according to the latest Circana data. Our growth this quarter was anchored by the fix channel. By leveraging our unique curation capabilities and expert stylists, we've leaned into head-to-toe outfitting and strategic category expansion.

This high-touch approach is resonating deeply. Both our women's and men's fix businesses grew double digits, contributing to a nearly 10% year-over-year increase in fix average order value, our 10th consecutive quarter of growth.

Stitch Fix still provides flexibility for its customers, allowing them to either order fixes or purchase directly on demand, which it calls its "freestyle" channel. Yet it's becoming clear that there is a subset of customers that is willing to pay Stitch Fix's premium for access to its stylist-curated Fixes, which is how the company can buck the weaker macro to gain market share.

The company notes that better product curation drove more successful Fix purchases. Some standout categories were activewear and athleisure (+37% y/y), outerwear (+26% y/y), and denim (+17% y/y). The company is also broadening its selection beyond clothing, with footwear sales growing 33% y/y and men's and women's accessories up 51% y/y.

Stitch Fix also isn't ignoring profitability. Gross margins remained stable at 43.6%. That said, we do note that the company leaned in slightly on advertising, spending 8.5% of its revenue on advertising versus 7.8% in the year-ago Q2. This led to adjusted EBITDA margins peeling back slightly, down 40bps y/y to 4.7%.

Still, we note adjusted EBITDA in nominal dollars was flat y/y - and given the carnage in the retail/consumer space, I'm more than willing to trade less than a point of margin to produce accelerating, industry-beating growth. We note as well that Stitch Fix has eked out $8.9 million of positive FCF in the first half of FY26, a marked improvement versus -$9.5 million of FCF burn in the prior-year period.

Risks and key takeaways

Of course, there are also risks that we absolutely need to keep in mind here. Stitch Fix is working against a tough consumer economy, and though that hasn't prevented its recent improvements in growth rates, the macro could worsen in calendar 2026 (especially with skyrocketing gas prices) and dent recent sales progress. Also, the retail environment is becoming increasingly more promotional, which may force Stitch Fix to give up more of its margins to keep pricing closer in line with peers.

That said, we think these risks are already priced into the stock at ~4x forward EBITDA. Again, I wouldn't venture a substantially large position here, but in my view, it's appropriate to nibble at this stock at current levels.

data.title: Nova Ltd.: A Stretched Valuation Amid An Unpriced Geopolitical Storm
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Nova Ltd. (Nasdaq:NVMI) has been a leader in the semiconductor manufacturing space since the mid-1990s. In this current AI-driven chip boom, Nova is well-positioned. By expanding its leadership in the metrology and calibration hardware niche and bolstered by the adoption of its Metrion platform at the two largest chipmakers, Nova has secured its position as a crucial player in the semiconductor wafer manufacturing ecosystem.

FY2025 saw record revenues, up 31% YoY, and a 60% increase in advanced packaging revenues. Management's guidance for growth in 2026, along with trends in Gate-All-Around (GAA) architecture, and strong demand for High Bandwidth Memory (HBM), support a strong investment outlook for Nova. Especially in advanced packaging, with SK Hynix, Inc. (HXSCL), Samsung Electronics Co. (SSNLF), and other peers running fabs at full capacity.

The company’s growth has not been overlooked by investors. The stock has surged over 119% in the last 52 weeks, and is trading at a forward P/E of 43x vs. 35x for peer KLA Corporation (Nasdaq:KLAC) and 32x for Onto Innovation Inc. (NYSE:ONTO). Since it serves about one-fifth of the customers of KLA and Onto Innovation, it carries a premium valuation. At the current valuation, much of the near-term upside is priced in. Though the mid- to long-term risk/reward calculus is balanced in our view, the short-term upside is not compelling. Therefore, we rate Nova a Hold at this time.

Business Overview

Competing in the metrology segment of the semiconductor industry, Nova designs, develops, and markets optical, materials, and chemical metrology equipment for measuring dimensions, chemical composition, and physical properties at multiple points in the semiconductor fabrication process. Without metrology, chipmakers cannot know if their devices are within specification. Without precision, yield collapses.

Nova's high-end product family, the Metrion, is based on the company's secondary ion mass spectrometry (SIMS) technology. The Metrion product family is popular for silicon structure profiling, and element and compound identification in advanced logic and memory.

Nova is well-positioned to benefit from two major shifts in the industry. First, process node advancement is moving toward Gate-All-Around (GAA) architecture, a fundamental transistor redesign where the gate wraps around the channel instead of sitting atop it. This requires more precise measurements of smaller and more complicated structures. Second, advanced chipmakers are stacking dies and using chiplets to maximize efficiency and performance, driving up the demand for metrology solutions in manufacturing. Both trends are supportive of Nova's platform.

In early 2025, Nova completed the acquisition of the Germany-based supplier Sentronics Metrology for approximately $60 million. The addition will strengthen the company's advanced packaging and 3D interconnect measurement capabilities, addressing a segment of customers that have been overlooked with legacy metrology tools.

Financials

In FY2025, the company generated $880.6 million in revenue (up 31% from FY2024's $672.4 million). As demand for chips has surged, many ancillary manufacturers in the supply chain have benefited. Due to the importance of Nova’s technology in the fabrication process, it’s no surprise that they are better positioned than most. The saying, "during a gold rush, sell shovels,” comes to mind.

Scaling in FY2025 was systematic by quarter. Q4 revenue of $222.6 million was up 14% YoY and exceeded management guidance, showing tremendous demand.

Gross margin was 57% for FY2024 and FY2025, slightly higher than FY2023's 56%. We believe this is a positive sign that Nova is not sacrificing margin for growth, and reflects pricing power and operating leverage.

GAAP operating margin was 28.78%, and non-GAAP operating margin was 33%. GAAP net income was $259.2 million, with EPS up 38% YoY. Non-GAAP net income was $282.6 million, or $8.62 per share.

With over $1.6 billion of cash and equivalents as of Q4, the balance sheet looks strong. The company was able to close on a very favorable debt financing round in September of last year, raising $750 million in 0% convertible notes due 2030. This extra capital gives Nova increased flexibility, and at terms most companies wouldn’t dream of, relative to market interest rates. The debt-to-equity ratio is 0.50, and the net cash per share is $26.24. The company generates solid free cash flow (to the tune of $218 million), and its R&D spend is appropriately 15% of revenue.

Management guidance estimates Q1 2026 revenues of $222-$232 million and GAAP EPS of $1.90 to $2.02. For the near term, we expect this revenue and earnings trend to continue.

Consensus projections anticipate FY2026 revenue of $1.07 billion (21% YoY increase), and FY2026 adjusted EPS of $10.46, or 31% above FY2025’s GAAP EPS of $7.96. FY2027 revenue is projected to increase 19% to $1.273 billion, up from $1.07 billion for FY2026. Management predicted that WFE spending would grow in the low double digits and that Nova would outperform the market.

Valuation

Nova's market capitalization, based on its 31.78 million shares outstanding and its current share price of $445, is ~$14 billion. In the past 52 weeks, the stock went from a low of $154 to a high of $507, a 229% increase. The latest price is roughly 11% below the Wall Street consensus target of $497, per Seeking Alpha, suggesting analysts see room to run from here.

Valuation metrics also show a financial mismatch compared to peers. The forward P/E multiple is 43x, considerably above its competition, even the largest competitor, KLA Corp., a $199 billion company with a 35x forward multiple. Onto Innovation is a $10.5 billion peer with a 32x forward multiple. Even the $7.4 billion Camtek (Nasdaq:CAMT) has a 46x forward multiple. Like many companies in the semiconductor space, there appears to be some valuation bloat in this category.

On an EV/revenue basis, Nova and KLA are trading at ~15x. But Nova is up against the $199 billion KLA with ~62% gross margins and its global customer base. After all, Nova's valuation implies that it will eventually trade at KLA's revenue and margins. But that will likely be years from now, and that's not guaranteed.

The PEG ratio of 2.92 underlines investor expectations for Nova’s future earnings growth. Since a PEG above 2.0 often means substantial growth is already reflected in the price, this valuation context implies that unless Nova outperforms, further upside may be limited without expansion in valuation multiples.

With a trailing P/E of 55x and a forward P/E of 43x based on expected EPS growth in FY2026, there is a whole lot of growth already baked into the stock. So much so that any misstep in execution or change in guidance would likely see heavy downward pressure on the stock.

The table shows that Nova was trading at a forward PE premium to KLA despite being only one-thirteenth the size. However, gross margins, while less than KLA's 62%, were similar. EV-to-revenue is the same as KLA's. Nova has a more concentrated customer base compared to KLA (in terms of the top 10 customers, for example), and while its new Germany facility adds chemical metrology capacity, most production remains concentrated in Israel.

Onto makes a telling comparison: growing 27% faster than Nova yet trading at an 11-point forward earnings discount to Nova at 32x versus Nova’s 43x. It also has lower customer concentration and greater geographic diversity. Camtek, which trades at a forward multiple of 46x, is smaller and considerably less liquid and so would not be a good precedent to use as a basis to value Nova.

Given current fundamentals, we think Nova's shares are closer to 32-35x forward earnings (~$335-$366 per share). The forward P/E multiple assumes it's either going to have to keep expanding its multiple over time, expand its margins to levels similar to KLA, or gain meaningful share in what will be a rapidly expanding TAM.

Catalysts and Growth Drivers

GAA is no longer hypothetical, with the two largest global chipmakers having adopted the Metrion platform to manufacture GAA. These customers are going to ramp up their volumes in an accelerating way as they move from pilot production to full production. As the processes get more complex with each node, these customers cannot skip metrology.

Advanced packaging revenue was up 60% YoY and now comprises around 20% of product revenue. The increased use of chiplet architectures and advanced interconnect technologies has allowed chip makers to continue to improve performance beyond scaling the process node. The Sentronics acquisition is thus really much more about this segment. In two or three years, advanced packaging could be 30-35% of revenue, or around double its current share.

Memory markets for DRAM and HBM are heavily utilized as memory chipmakers SK Hynix, Samsung, and others run memory fabs at full capacity and extensively invest in new memory nodes. Particularly, HBM is a big opportunity for the memory industry, and the ramp is just beginning. Nova gives these customers what they need.

Weak NAND flash markets have been a concern for years, but improvements appear to be on the horizon. If NAND capex rises sharply in 2026-2027, Nova will benefit from a fill-in ramp due to customer qualification.

Management alluded to a meaningful acceleration in H2 2026 beyond its low-double-digit WFE guidance. If achieved, 2027 would see high-20s growth and reset the growth narrative for the sector, supporting current valuations. We'll be watching Q2 2026 earnings closely to see if this is a real acceleration.

Risks

The stock has increased 119% in the last 52 weeks and is experiencing the benefits of the current AI boom. While this has been a great run for the stock, the company is equally at the mercy of AI-driven market sentiment, for good and bad. While still below the Wall Street consensus target of $497, the SA Quant rating is a HOLD with a D valuation grade. Thus, under the PEG of 2.92, valuation has little room to expand. Any miss, any slowdown in customer capex, or any macro sentiment change in growth stocks will almost certainly have an outsized effect on the stock price.

It is also important to note geopolitical risk factors unique to the company. Nova is based in Rehovot, Israel, and manufactures its devices there. As of March 2026, Israel and the US are engaged in an active and escalating war with Iran. This is not an abstract risk; it has real operational consequences, especially when infrastructure is under threat.

The conflict has raised the price of energy for the whole world, with the price of Brent crude oil above $110 a barrel, as opposed to $80 a barrel in 2023. Semiconductor fabs are energy-intensive, and the increase in electricity costs has added 15-25% to customers' costs. High fab operating expenses, such as increased electricity costs, may delay chipmakers from buying equipment, causing spending to be deferred or canceled altogether.

Risk is also present due to disruption of critical supply chains: Qatar's Ras Laffan helium plant, responsible for around a third of global supply, was damaged in recent Iranian strikes. Helium is essential for semiconductor manufacture and operation. Further price increases are expected if the plant halts production and there is increased demand for helium from fabs. Israel and Jordan are responsible for two-thirds of the world's bromine supply, which is used in many semiconductor processes.

Nova is directly exposed to operational risk because of the concentration of its manufacturing facilities, While the company recently opened a chemical metrology facility in Germany (doubling that division's capacity), the bulk of Nova’s production, including its core optical and materials metrology lines, remains in Israel. Military escalation, including fighting (excl. attacks on factories) and staff taken from product development, leads to uncertainty. Mobilization of reservists caused delays to designs in 2024. More importantly, it would pull engineers away from production, research, and development. Also, wage inflation, due to shortages of specialist engineering talent exacerbated by security problems, affected Nova's cost base (wages are roughly 35% of its cost base).

The conflict has reduced semiconductor demand. SK Hynix and Samsung have lost about $200 billion in combined market capitalization, and their stock prices have dropped in the wake of the conflict. Nova's growth target for 2026 could be missed if memory chipmakers cut capex in response to weak demand or their own weakening balance sheets. Memory accounts for roughly 25% of Nova’s current product revenue, but we estimate memory represents 40-50% of its total addressable market.

Also worth considering is that Nova, like most Western manufacturers of chip-related equipment, is subject to restrictions on sales to Chinese customers.

The $750 million in convertibles due in 2030 carry a conversion price of roughly $320 per share, which would add approximately 2.34 million shares (7.4% dilution) to the existing 31.78 million basic shares. Nova did enter capped call transactions at $415 per share to limit dilution, but with the stock now trading above that cap, the effective offset is reduced. Shareholders should evaluate the conversion mechanics to account for dilution long-term.

Wafer Fabrication Equipment (WFE) spending is cyclical; the current cycle is driven by AI, node transitions, and strong demand for memory. Though cycles do cycle, in FY2023, chip manufacturing equipment demand collapsed overnight, leading to a 9% decline in revenue. If capex peaks in 2026-27, Nova's revenue growth would decelerate sharply. The guidance of steady WFE growth looks overly optimistic given the semiconductor industry's track record of guiding below consensus.

Conclusion

We believe Nova Ltd. is one of the highest-quality semiconductor equipment companies in the world. For investors, Nova brings a lot to the table: a sound business model, a solid tech pipeline, favorable tailwinds from GAA and advanced packaging, and an established management team. We also believe the company's target of $1.3 billion in revenue by 2027 is credible.

Despite these factors, in our view, the stock has outpaced its fundamentals. At ~$445, a forward price-earnings ratio of ~43+ puts the company's $14+ billion market cap in a likely unsustainable range, at least in the short-term. Our analysis indicates upside is limited unless earnings growth accelerates above consensus estimates.

We prefer to wait for Q2 results to confirm management's thesis for the second half of 2026. The geopolitical picture is still not priced in adequately in our view, and could cause tangible downside risk.

At Avalon Capital Research, we rate Nova Ltd. a HOLD.

data.title: MTU Aero Engines: This 17% Drop Is A Buying Opportunity
data.description:
MTU Aero Engines AG (MTUAY, MTUAF) has lost 17% of its value since my last report and that is definitely not the type of performance I am looking for in a strong buy rated name. I believe that MTU Aero Engines is still looking attractive for the long term, and the share price decline is more in sympathy with an industry that saw its valuation levels rise substantially rather than an immediate concern for MTU Aero Engine’s business. However, we also note that the company’s earnings presentation included one particular slide that captures a justified concern that investors may have. In this report, I discuss the company’s earnings for 2025, assess the guidance and update my price target.

MTU Aero Engines Had A Good Year Despite GTF Pressures

For 2025, MTU had guided for revenues of €8.6-€8.8 billion against an initial guidance of €8.3-€8.5 billion. The company ended the year with €8.72 billion in sales marking 16% growth in sales. EBIT grew 29% to €1.35 billion with margins growing from 14% to 15.5%. The company had initially guided mid-teens growth and later upped that to mid-twenties. So, the nearly 30% growth is extremely strong. The company’s 2030 targets were 14.5%-15.5% and we see that MTU reached that years ahead of the planning. Net income increased 27% to €968 million while free cash flow more than doubled to €378 million despite €360 million in GTF related compensation to customers. The GTF engine issues are not caused by MTU, but as a risk-sharing partner on the program they are impacted by the issues.

MTU Aero Engines saw its OEM (Original Equipment Manufacturer) sales increase 13.6% to €2.875 billion driven by 18% growth in OEM revenues to €2.26 billion while OEM military revenue remained flat at €614 billion. EBIT grew 43% to €873 million marking a margin improvement from 24.2% to 30.4%. The growth was driven by a favorable OEM mix. We do note that organic commercial OEM sales performance was strong but still was somewhat behind of expectations.

Commercial MRO sales increased 18% to €5.96 billion with adjusted EBIT growing 9% to €478 million as margins contracted 70 basis points to 8%. Margins were lower driven by ramp up costs of the GTF facility in Fort Worth, expansion investments and a higher share of GTF in the sales mix. In 2025, 40% of sales were GTF MRO revenues and that is important to keep in mind as these sales are dilutive to the margin profile.

Military MRO is not reported within a separate segment but given the structure of military contracts they are part of the OEM reporting and the company noted that performance was softer than expected due to supply chain issues, which explains the absence of growth in 2025.

MTU Aero Engines Guides For Softer Margins With High Single Digit Sales Growth

2025 was a year for MTU Aero Engines with strong growth, but defense was flat due to supply chain issues while some expected commercial business did not materialize. Furthermore, there were ramp up costs and GTF related customer compensations.

For 2026, the company expects sales between €9.2 billion and €9.7 billion which indicates 8.6% growth at the mid-point of the range. Commercial OEM revenues are expected to be up mid to high teens while commercial OEM spare parts are expected to grow low to mid-teens with the military business up mid-teens and commercial MRO sales up low to mid-teens. New engine deliveries, the GTF ramp and production rate increases on the Boeing 787 program and preparations for the service entry of the Boeing 777X should drive commercial OEM sales while aircraft shortages should drive demand for commercial spare parts sales including higher GTF spare parts sales and in the military business growth platforms include the Eurojet EJ200 engine and the T408 used on the CH-53K helicopter. MRO sales are expected to grow driven by higher GE90 business and GTF MRO.

EBIT is guided between €1.35 billion and €1.45 billion marking 3.7% growth marking a 70 basis point margin compression at the mid-point of the range driven by the dilutive impact that higher GTF sales have on the business and continued ramp up costs for new facilities.

The company has not explicitly guided on free cash flow, but if we use the same ratio between EBIT and net income, we get to €1.01 billion in net income for 2026. With the cash conversion ratio of 40%-45% this would indicate €450-€550 million. If the company gets to that point it would indicate 19%-45% growth which is impressive if we consider that the company will still have a $250 drag from customer compensations and credit on the GTF program as well as working capital build and ramp up costs. So, what we are seeing as extremely strong cash flow growth in 2026 is still not a fully normalized level.

The Part That May Have Investors Concerned

MTU had its earnings call prior to the war and in their presentation they provided a look at the expectations for GDP and RPK growth in 2026. The expected elasticity of passenger growth is roughly 1.6x. So for each percentage growth in GDP there will be 1.6x growth in RPK. A prolonged conflict in the Middle East could shave off 1 to 2 points from the GDP outlook, which could in RPK growth to fall to 1.8%-3.6%. If the war ends today, it would have a 50 basis point impact which already seems to have a 80 basis point impact on the projections from MTU Aero Engines. There is no sensitivity gauge on how this would affect MTU, but the rationale is that lower RPK growth could affect demand for airplanes and maintenance and parts. I do not believe that we will see a substantial reduction in new build deliveries since jet makers are years behind on delivery schedules, but some RPK growth underpinning MRO and spare parts growth could be under pressure and that in combination with the high valuations for aerospace and defense names has put pressure on MTU Aero Engines stock. I do believe that the falling USD against the EUR has an incremental EBIT uplift, but we note that the company is 80% hedged at an average rate of €1=$1.13 so for this year the upside is limited through the currency hedges.

MTU Aero Engines Looks Like An Even Stronger Buy Today

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Price targets are based on the ordinary shares listed in Germany. Investors should keep in mind that the ticker MTUAF represents the ordinary shares, while the ticker MTUAY represents 0.5x ordinary shares.

The decline in the share prices has resulted in significant undervaluation for MTU Aero Engines. The company’s share prices were already trading at a discount compared its future year earnings potential. That gap has widened meaning the strong buy rating actually becomes even stronger. There indeed is a macro risk, but we do note that financial performance is set to improve substantially with almost 10% annual growth in sales, 6.9% growth in EBITDA reflecting lower margin GTF platform sales and 34.5% growth in free cash flow generation. The company was extremely close to a net cash position in 2025. Against the 2025 earnings, the stock is undervalued by 46% which would bring the price target to $524.77. Even if we only assign half of the upside to account for macroeconomic weakness and do not include any of the upside in the years ahead there would still be a strong 26% upside. So, I remain bullish on MTU Aero Engines. There is a macroeconomic risk driven by the situation in the Middle East but we also note that the stock sold off in sympathy with the aerospace and defense peer group while MTU Aero Engines trades at a significant discount to that peer group. In other words: An already undervalued named became even cheaper.

In terms of margins, there is not a lot of upside. In fact, EBITDA margins are expected to settle around 19% as lower margin GTF sales dilute the group margins. The cash flow performance shows strong growth in the years ahead driven by current headwinds on the GTF program and supply chain issues fading. At the same time, capital intensity will increase against cash flow and double from 4% to 8% against revenues. The company is expected to enter in a net cash position this year meaning that while there is some macroeconomic uncertainty, we are looking at an undervalued name with growth prospects and a fortress balance sheet.

Conclusion: MTU Aero Engines Stock Is Oversold

Looking at the financial performance, we note that even with some soft spots and program overhangs the financial performance in 2025 was impressive. In the years ahead, the overhangs should dissolve showing the true performance of MTU Aero Engines. Of course, the macroeconomic outlook provides some uncertainty, but I do believe that MTU Aero Engines is positioned well for long-term growth providing an attractive long-term entry point on the current pullback in the share prices.

data.title: Acuity's Loss Makes Buying A Bright Idea (Upgrade)
data.description:
April 2nd was a really rough day for shareholders of Acuity Inc. (AYI). The stock dropped over 7.5% in late afternoon trading after management reported, prior to the market opening, financial results for the second quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Although revenue and earnings both increased year over year, they fell short of analysts’ expectations. This is disappointing to say the least. But there is some good news here. And this is the fact that the stock is now trading cheaper than it was. In fact, based on recent growth and how cheap shares appear to be at the moment, I would actually argue that upgrading it from the Hold I rated it in January of this year to a very soft Buy makes sense.

The picture finally looks good enough

In the past, I have been rather neutral about Acuity. In my January article, for instance, I acknowledged that the business was achieving operational improvements. However, the stock was not yet cheap enough to justify anything other than a Hold rating. Since then, however, shares are down 17%. That's far worse than the 5.4% drop that the S&P 500 (SP500) experienced over the same window of time. It is worth noting that, since I originally rated it a Hold way back in March of 2022, the stock is up just a bit less than the market is, having risen 44.2% compared to the 50.7% rise that the market enjoyed. But even back then, the stock looked a bit more expensive from a valuation standpoint than I wanted it to be.

You would think, judging by recent underperformance, that the company was experiencing issues. But I would challenge that narrative. Year over year for the second quarter of the 2026 fiscal year, revenue grew from $1.01 billion to $1.06 billion. While that meant that the company still fell short of expectations that analysts had given to the tune of $25.1 million, it still represents a nice increase. We saw a similar thing when it came to profitability. Earnings per share jumped from $2.45 to $3.09. But that ended up being $0.08 below what analysts were hoping for. Still, that meant a pretty nice jump in net profits from $77.5 million to $96.8 million. Meanwhile, adjusted earnings per share grew from $3.73 to $4.14. That ended up being $0.14 below what analysts were expecting. Still, that meant a rise in adjusted net income from $118.1 million to $129.9 million. The chart above shows that, as revenue and profits increased, cash flows for the company grew also.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first half of the 2026 fiscal year compared to the same time of 2025. Revenue, profits, and cash flows, grew without exception here. And the fact of the matter is that we have its smallest operating segment, known as its Acuity Intelligent Spaces segment, to thank for this. This is a fascinating operating unit, with its emphasis being on using technology to make spaces more productive. This materializes in not only software, but also controls, sensors, and the like, that help to regulate things like heating, ventilation, air conditioning, refrigeration, lighting, shades, and even building access.

For the second quarter of the 2026 fiscal year, revenue for the segment was $248.1 million. That's 44.7% above the $171.5 million the company reported a year earlier. Management attributed this increase to the firm's acquisition of QSC, as well as because of higher sales of its Distech products. For context, in January of last year, management acquired QSC, which operates as a producer of audio, video, and control solutions and services. This was a rather large purchase, costing the company $1.2 billion at the time. This was not the only purchase that it made. Later in the year, as an example, the firm picked up a sports lighting startup by the name of M3 Innovation. But that was such a small acquisition that management did not need to disclose the specifics of the transaction.

As revenue for this segment improved, profitability increased also. Operating income skyrocketed from $9.9 million to $28.3 million. And for the first half of the 2026 fiscal year as a whole, the segment generated revenue of $505.5 million and operating profits of $65.3 million. A year earlier, these numbers were substantially lower at $245 million and $20.7 million, respectively.

Although this has been great to see, there has been some weakness when it comes to the firm's largest operating segment. This is none other than Acuity Brands Lighting, which focuses on various lighting solutions products to customers, including those that fall under the intelligent lighting solutions category. But unfortunately, revenue here dropped year over year, contracting from $840.6 million to $817.4 million. Management blamed this on lower sales involving its direct sales network, with that in turn attributable in part to project business that did not recur this year after occurring last year. Naturally, this would mean a drop in profits, with operating income falling from $130.3 million to $125.1 million. The chart above shows weakness for the first half of 2026 as well when it comes to revenue. But at least during this time, segment profits improved marginally.

I would say that, even though financial performance fell short of expectations for the quarter, the data reported by management was still pretty bullish. Unfortunately, we should expect further weakness when it comes to the Acuity Brands Lighting segment, with management forecasting a decline in revenue for it that will be flat at best and down at a low single digit rate at worst. However, we should still anticipate the Acuity Intelligent Spaces segment to report growth of between a low teens to mid-teens rate. Obviously, acquisition activities are helping here.

However, management also pointed out that Distech’s Eclypse platform performed well during this time, especially because of the release of its Eclypse retrofit solution. For the uninitiated, the company’s Eclypse platform refers to a combination of hardware and software that allows customers to select certain monitoring and controlling functionality when it comes to individual equipment or for centrally managing smaller buildings. This is all part of the smart building revolution that we are slowly seeing. Clearly, the business has a nice hold over a piece of this space.

In all likelihood, we will see some challenges throughout this year. Management mentioned, for instance, that Acuity Brands Lighting projects are seeing releases at slower paces. But because conversion rates are around the same and because the company is benefiting from non-repeat projects, they did say that they don't believe that they are in a down market. On the other hand, I have been arguing since August of last year that the U.S. is nearing a recession. And if I do turn out to be accurate on this, some weakness would be anticipated.

Management said that revenue would rise from $4.35 billion last year to between $4.7 billion and $4.9 billion this year. Taking the midpoint of guidance for adjusted earnings per share, we should get a rise in adjusted net profits from $569.8 million to around $619.4 million this year. If we project out results based on what we have seen already for the 2026 fiscal year, we would anticipate adjusted operating cash flow of $728.6 million and EBITDA of $952.7 million. Using these estimates, as well as the results that we saw for the 2025 fiscal year, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are priced right now. Honestly, the forward figures here sit between the fair value and undervalued range as I typically describe it.

But the most important point here is that the stock is getting substantially cheaper based on these projections. On top of this, as that chart illustrates, the firm is also cheaper than other companies that have similarities to it. On a price to earnings basis and on an EV to EBITDA basis, it ended up being the cheapest of the group. And when it came to the price to operating cash flow approach, only one firm was cheaper than it is.

Takeaway

In my mind, I would argue that while the market was not necessarily irrational to push shares down because management fell short of expectations for the quarter, that the overall drop that the stock has experienced now offers a good entry point. I am cautious about the economy more broadly. But with the rapid growth that we have seen with the Acuity Intelligent Spaces business, on top of how cheap the stock now looks, I believe that upgrading it to a very soft Buy is the right choice here.

data.title: Anfield Energy Is A High-Risk, High-Reward Play For Uranium
data.description:
Anfield Energy (AEC) has been one of the top-performing stocks of the entire stock market in the last 12 months. The stock has surged 93%, and thus it has outperformed the S&P 500, which has gained 17%, by an impressive margin. The outstanding performance of Anfield Energy has resulted from the bright outlook of nuclear power and the ideal positioning of the company to benefit from the expected boom of nuclear power. The big question is whether the stock remains attractive after such a steep rally.

Business overview

Anfield Energy is engaged in the exploration, development, and production of mineral properties in the U.S. It is primarily focused on uranium, vanadium, and gold properties. Uranium is the key input of nuclear reactors, while vanadium is a vital metal in many industries, such as construction, aerospace, and automotive.

Nuclear power was suffering from negative public sentiment for decades due to two catastrophic accidents, namely the accident at Chernobyl, Ukraine, in 1986 and the accident in Fukushima, Japan, in 2011. Due to these two accidents, the global energy produced by nuclear plants has remained essentially flat over the last two decades.

Source: World Nuclear Association

However, the global energy landscape is currently going through a major transition. The demand for energy is likely to grow significantly in the upcoming years thanks to the secular boom of AI data centers. As nearly all countries are doing their best to curtail the consumption of fossil fuels, the incremental demand should be covered with clean energy sources. Solar and wind power plants are clean sources of energy, but they are not 100% reliable, as they cannot generate energy on a continuous basis due to the volatile nature of weather. Therefore, nuclear power is widely viewed as the ideal solution, as it is both clean and continuous.

Uranium is the primary input of nuclear reactors, and hence the demand for uranium is likely to greatly increase in the upcoming years. In addition, supply is limited, as there has been very low investment in uranium exploration over the last two decades due to the aforementioned negative public sentiment over nuclear power. Therefore, the outlook for future uranium prices appears favorable.

Anfield Energy is ideally positioned to benefit from the expected boom in the global demand for uranium.

Source: Investor Presentation

One of its primary assets, the Shootaring Canyon Mill in Utah, is one of only three licensed uranium mills in the U.S. This asset is highly promising, as it is located in one of the most prolific production areas in the U.S. The company applied for a production restart to Utah’s Department of Environmental Quality and received a positive response in 2024. Whenever Anfield Energy resumes production at this site, it intends to increase the throughput capacity of the mill from 750 to 1000 tons per day.

Source: Investor Presentation

It is worth noting that the Shootaring Canyon Mill was producing uranium at some point in the early 80s, but it stopped production in 1982 due to weak uranium prices. It has remained idle for more than four decades, but it is about to return to operation thanks to the favorable outlook of the uranium business. Management expects the project to return to operation in the second half of this year.

It is also important to note that the U.S. government approved the Velvet-Wood project of Anfield Energy via a fast-track 14-day review process last year. This project was the first uranium project to be approved via an emergency process, thus revealing the will of the U.S. government to do its best to enhance the domestic energy supplies. The ease of approval certainly bodes well for the growth prospects of Anfield Energy, as it is indicative of a very friendly political environment.

Notably, Uranium Energy (UEC), a much larger uranium producer with a market capitalization of $6.6 billion, doubled its stake in Anfield Energy last year and thus it now has a 32.4% stake in the company. This is undoubtedly a strong vote of confidence of Uranium Energy in the growth prospects of Anfield Energy.

Valuation

Anfield Energy has yet to generate revenues and earnings. As a result, the stock cannot be evaluated based on the well-known conventional metrics, such as price-to-earnings and price-to-sales ratios.

On the other hand, one can have an idea of the upside potential of the stock based on a rough comparison to Uranium Energy. According to the latest presentation of Anfield Energy, Uranium Energy has total uranium resources that are 7.5 times greater than those of Anfield Energy (365 vs. 49 Mlbs). It also has a market capitalization that is 65 times greater than that of Anfield Energy ($6.6 billion vs. $0.101 billion).

On the one hand, Uranium Energy is a more established and much less speculative company, and hence it warrants a high premium over Anfield Energy. On the other hand, it is evident that Anfield Energy has great upside potential if the favorable business landscape related to nuclear power remains in place.

Risks

Anfield Energy is different from other uranium producers, such as Uranium Energy and Cameco (CCJ), as the former is not generating revenues. It has posted losses in 9 of the last 10 years, and thus it has diluted its shareholders to a great extent to fund its obligations. To be sure, its share count has more than quadrupled over the last five years. If Anfield Energy executes its restart plan without any setbacks, it is likely to highly reward its shareholders. On the other hand, if its project is greatly delayed, it will have to issue many new shares, and thus its stock price is likely to come under pressure.

In the six months leading to September 2025 (this is the most recent report of the company), Anfield Energy reported a decrease of $4.5 million in its cash position. In other words, its cash burn rate is approximately $9.0 million per year. As the last cash position reported was $5.2 million, it was evident that the company would need to issue new shares. Indeed, Anfield Energy agreed to issue 50,000 new shares and 500,000 common share purchase warrants in January. Going forward, the company may need to dilute its shareholders further. Only the investors who are comfortable with this risk should consider buying the stock.

Final thoughts

Anfield Energy is a speculative stock, as it has yet to generate revenues. However, thanks to the highly promising prospects of nuclear energy amid a boom in energy demand from AI data centers, the stock appears attractive, particularly given the favorable stance of the U.S. government towards energy projects. Moreover, the aforementioned project at Shootaring Canyon Mill is expected to come online in just a few months, and hence there is minimal execution risk, in contrast to the execution risk of long-term exploration projects. Nevertheless, as the company has not generated any revenues or earnings yet, it carries an elevated amount of risk, and hence investors should allocate a limited portion of their capital to this stock.
