data.title: AIPI: From Capped Upside To Rangebound Income Opportunity
data.description:
In May last year, I had issued a Hold rating for the REX AI Equity Premium Income ETF (AIPI). At that time, and for a few months following that rating, everything tagged AI was being lapped up by the market. That environment has changed. The AI trade has cooled, macro pressures and stagflation risks aside. Importantly, the core objective for income seekers is to be able to generate optimal income, whether through an option writing route or through capital appreciation of the underlying.

Last year AIPI was not posing much upside from the AI rally because of the capped nature of the covered call writing. Income was still supportive because some upside capture was still at play, although despite the option income, the aggressive yields meant NAV erosion possibilities, which is bad news for future payout size. You can see the effect of smaller payouts over time in the dividend payout chart below. This is despite any meaningful change in yield levels. That effect only amplifies if the underlying portfolio stagnates.

Theoretically, the strategy does not need a sharp rally in the underlying. What is actually and optimally needed is a supportive portfolio as a minimum, which then opens up option income in rangebound markets. The total returns can, in such a regime, even outperform the underlying portfolio itself. That regime is here today — after a reset in the AI trade, we could see exactly the perfect markets for AIPI. One where the risks of a further downside is more limited than it was in May last year, and the odds of a sharp rally also doesn't look imminent. A rangebound market is where option income works best. In short, compared to my old thesis, where the upside opportunity loss was far higher than the drawdown mitigation, we have now moved into a more symmetric market for option writing. That moves AIPI to Buy territory.

Performance Since May

The below chart shows the total return trajectory for AIPI, with AIQ and QQQ as anchors to help contextualize. Neither of the anchors have the same exact underlying portfolio as AIPI, but AIQ should show an approximate path for total return investors invested in the AI theme (AIPI has a much smaller high conviction portfolio of ~25 stocks compared to AIQ's broader ~90 stock exposure), and QQQ shows an even broader AI path without AIPI's high conviction positioning.

The total returns chart shows how AIPI expectedly was unable to capture the AI trade's upside in a strong bull market till October 2025, something even a broader AI portfolio like AIQ could. AIPI's total return trajectory (implies payouts reinvested and option income embedded on top of portfolio gains) traced a very broad exposure to AI like QQQ in the bull run. That often means bad news for holding through the cycle - if one of the sharpest AI rallies only means tracking a broader AI portfolio (like QQQ), then in even slightly cooling markets, the downside will be far higher. This is also in line with what we see, AIPI's upside is QQQ but the downside is probably as high as AIQ, and this despite the option income.

That also means the expected NAV erosion starts showing up aggressively in flat to negative regimes for the AI trade. While the NAV erosion threat was broadly contained till October, we have now seen 20-30% NAV erosion. The performance of AIPI since May overall upholds the concerns I had around the time of the earlier thesis. For a strategy that at best retains NAV in one of the best times for its underlying portfolio; NAV erosion should be expected to be the norm in most market conditions.

That is unlikely to be fixed ahead unless yields are moderated or reinvested. But three things matter in addition to NAV erosion — one, the quality of income, two, the degree of NAV erosion and three, the asymmetry of upside and downside moves. On these parameters, AIPI seems to have moved into a better regime today. And all those themes are inter-related too. A cooler regime for AI valuations imply we are no longer staring at capturing say 2% upside when underlying holdings are rallying by 10%, yet staring at all the risks of cooling ahead (like we were in May to October 2025), with minimal cushion from option income in drawdowns. That makes it a more symmetrical setup for AIPI now. A flat rangebound outlook also means slower upsides are captured, option premiums add to the total returns and overall, the quality of income is option dominated (rather than a return of capital which is structurally not allowed to grow well either due to the covered call upside capping setup). Since the underlying is flat, NAV erodes by the difference of payouts versus option income. This is an improvement from the most recent drawdown regime where NAV erodes due to corrections in the underlying as well.

Why The Rangebound View

A look at AIPI's portfolio explains my rangebound view on the portfolio. Around 70% of the portfolio is in enablers and adopters, with high beta and uncertainties at the moment. The enablers exposure is primarily in semis and AI infrastructure, where late cycle concerns are emerging. The trough in hyperscaler CapEx could indeed turn out to be higher than what markets fear at this point and AI could be the multi-decade secular tailwind it was positioned as, at the start of the cycle. Even if cyclicality returns in the semis space, we could see that later than the immediate next year too. But the odds of a multiple expansion definitely looks less possible at the moment. We do not have to move too far beyond the NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) stagnation over the past few months to verify this rangebound outlook in the semis space. I have written about how the tech heavyweights will likely remain rangebound unless earnings upgrades return, in a separate article on the Schwab US Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG).

The next biggest exposure is in an even more uncertain bucket of adopters, dominated primarily by SaaS companies. This is where markets are gauging the effects of a steady state AI dynamics, commoditization risks and pricing pressures thereafter. While the bulk of corrections have already happened here, there is no urgency to rerate the pack (except a few pockets), unless certainty arrives. And that uncertainty will linger for a few quarters at least as the tech world evolves through AI's capabilities and positioning.

The hyperscaler pack is probably the most resilient group in AIPI's portfolio, but it is underweight compared to semis and SaaS. These companies are also going through their own CapEx drag pressures, but monetization uncertainties are not as binary as in the case of SaaS or cyclical as in the case of semis. The bigger question for this layer is the return on capex and the economics of the AI monetization phase. Some of the companies in this pack, like Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) or Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) have corrected quite a bit too acknowledging the high spend regime. So I expect this layer to be relatively stable, but we are certainly not looking at immediate upsides, at least at a 2025-like pace.

How Well is AIPI Set Up?

AIPI writes calls on individual holdings rather than at an index level. I downloaded the full holdings as of April 2nd 2026 and saw covered calls written for almost the full holding notional value for the top 10 holdings. There are some mechanisms like a laddered call setup which means some upside capture is not ruled out (with some strikes far OTM), but this is an aggressive option writing setup as such.

That aggressive setup with a bit of upside room for underlying stocks is the perfect recipe for the current rangebound outlook, as we have discussed before. It ensures the portfolio generates flat to marginally positive returns over the next few quarters and the option layer generates decent income, especially if conditions remain as volatile as we have seen over the past month.

A Buy Call for the Next Few Quarters

A Buy for an income plan does not necessarily mean buying the underlying portfolio. So the AI tag does not help AIPI's thesis as much as it looks like. Income strategies should look at a moderately bullish thesis in the underlying, in the long term which AI comes with. That thesis is strengthened after massive corrections in the SaaS space and some cooling in semis and hyperscaler stocks. And beyond a point, such strategies do not need a sharp bull run versus a moderate one - beyond a threshold, the upside does not matter much (especially if it is within a short period of time). To that end, I was wary of the disproportionate downside risks in May last year, without commensurate benefits of the sharp upside getting captured. That thesis has now turned to a Buy. I expect the underlying portfolio to now be in an ideal position to keep the overall upside under check. Downside risks are not as high as earlier. And the option income should be able to add value than just cap upsides.

data.title: Texas Pacific Land Corporation: Still Too Expensive
data.description:
The last article on Texas Pacific Land Corporation (TPL) mentioned that the stock was extremely expensive for the growth characteristics of the company. Since then, the Iran situation that includes effectively closing the Strait of Hormuz has basically sent everything in the industry much higher. However, such issues rarely last long. Because the price-earnings ratio remains very high, this particular idea has a lot more downside potential when things settle down than do many of my other choices.

Stock Price

The last article showed the Quant system ranking this as a Strong Sell. But the Iranian situation changed that ranking because of the stock price action. The Quant system now has the stock as a Hold.

It is very easy to see the effect that the Iranian situation has had on the stock price from the chart above. That was definitely an unknown at the time the last article was written.

However, this is not an industry that is known for price-earnings ratios of 52 for really much of any reason. This stock has long been an exception to much of the industry. As such, it is really just a question as to when the stock price will value the business in the same fashion as much of the rest of the industry.

Pricing like this can persist for a while. The Iranian situation is far from settled. Therefore, commodity prices can go a lot higher before they inevitably decline. The timing of anything in the future is very much unsettled right now in the eyes of the market.

The Business

The company has two main revenue streams currently with some future possibilities.

The land it has it farms out on a royalty basis to form one main revenue stream. This business is basically an upstream business without the major risks of upstream like dry holes or the expenses of drilling and completion. The company largely makes its money as a passive entity as it does not control development of the resources on its leases. Keep in mind that this is a simple overview.

A little later came the water business. That is more a midstream business of providing water services to companies developing its resources on an as needed basis.

Now there is talk of some other ventures like possibly some data centers in the future. But right now, the two main businesses were already described.

Earnings

For the latest fiscal year, earnings came in at roughly $7 per share. For much of the industry, earnings like that would justify a stock price at best of possibly $70.

This is a cyclical industry. Therefore, the market will generally value a company based upon its average profitability. Now thanks to unconventional upstream and the water business, this is a cyclical growth story. But this industry is not the tech industry. So large price-earnings ratios as is seen here are at best few and far between. Additionally, they rarely last.

This is obviously a very profitable business as shown by the large percentage that Net Income would be of Revenue. But the growth rate for earnings (as shown above) is in the single digits.

Earnings growth can be influenced by declining commodity prices as was likely the case in fiscal year 2024. Much of the valuation process depends upon whether the market focuses on production per share growth or earnings per share growth. But a big consideration is that this seems to be a momentum-based market that may focus on positive comparisons (period) with no excuses. A cyclical downturn may not be tolerated by the market.

What has changed since the last article is the ascendency of commodity prices. Now that could make for roughly a doubling of earnings per share (at least while it lasts). But it also brings about a price-earnings ratio contraction because the market prices these (lower or less valuable) for above average earnings during periods of high commodity prices (because those great periods do not last).

I am thinking that the Iranian situation will mark a cyclical top for the industry even if that cyclical top does not last long. But much will depend upon the supply of various commodities after the situation ends (and how fast damages get repaired as well).

Complicating this is the fact that Venezuela is now in a position to rebuild its production as well. As usual, timing would be uncertain.

But it does seem like the currently high prices will be replaced at some point in the future by a period of abundance and probably low commodity prices.

Realize that this is a very low visibility industry where things can change very fast with little warning.

Balance Sheet

If you go to the SEC filings on the company website (page 4) and click on the 10-K, it is easy to see that the company has no long-term debt. This is typical for companies with a royalty business. But that means that the company has both the cash flow and the financial flexibility to pursue new businesses with its land or to return the money to shareholders through share buybacks or dividends.

Risk

Any upstream business, including a royalty business is dependent upon the level of commodity prices. For this reason, it is best to consider any company with a royalty business as a variable distribution entity.

To help defend the dividend there is a stock repurchase program that can be eliminated first in the event of a severe and sustained downturn that would necessitate such actions.

Similarly, the level of commodity prices also influences the general upstream activity level and hence the per share production growth. An unforeseen severe and sustained commodity price decline could also eliminate the business growth for a while as well.

The opposite is true as well. A sustained upturn in commodity prices would enhance (at least temporarily) growth prospects.

The water business is a bit steadier because as production ages, the wells often produce more water. This counter cyclical nature would partially offset the lower need for water for fracking during a cyclical downturn.

Summary

The current price-earnings ratio is not justified by the earnings growth rate. There may be a one-time large earnings boost as commodity prices rise to accommodate the situation that arises as a result of the Iranian crisis. But once the Iranian crisis is settled there is likely to be a cyclical downturn at least on the horizon.

For the time being, the growth rate does not appear to support a price-earnings ratio of 53. As commodity prices rise, cyclical stocks often have lower price-earnings ratios because the market knows that high commodity prices do not last in a free market. The cure for high prices is high prices in a free market.

While I personally believe that a price-earnings ratio of 10 is plenty for the financial characteristics shown in the article. The final number when we get there could well depend upon inflation, interest rates, and whether or not the industry is a market favorite.

Let us assume that commodity prices are high enough to justify an earnings rate of $15 per share annually while the Iranian situation lasts. That would price the stock around $150. That is far below the current price. It will not get any better when the Iranian situation is over unless we get another surprise.

But I have no way of justifying a price-earnings ratio of even 20 under the most optimistic scenarios.

Therefore, for me, this remains a Strong Sell because I believe that I can find far cheaper alternatives that have the same financial characteristics or better.

That does not mean that the current situation will not see the price go higher. But it does mean that for me, the current valuation is not sustainable long-term. Therefore, it is very possible that a trading profit opportunity exists but not a buy-and-hold opportunity.

data.title: Workday: SaaSpocalypse Selloff Creates A Golden Opportunity
data.description:
With fears of an AI-induced software apocalypse hitting markets, Workday, Inc. (WDAY) clearly hasn't been spared in the carnage. As shown in the chart below, the stock has seen losses of over 40% in the past six months and the bulk of those declines have occurred this year. Interestingly, despite being a tech focused analyst, I haven't initiated coverage on this SaaS giant yet. Given the current state of things, I believe that now is the right time to take a look at their fundamentals and give readers my analysis.

Below, little signs of a SaaSpocalypse are found. Growth actually accelerated in their Q4 and margins expanded. Moving forward, guidance for FY2027 was decent but slower margin expansion may be a near term risk as the company continues to invest in AI. For long term investors this may actually be a good sign. With the forward P/E now at clearly depressed levels, I have decided to initiate Workday at a buy rating as the end of the line for the company is really nowhere in sight.

AI Integration

While the market is fretting about AI disruption for Workday, in reality the company has integrated AI deep within their enterprise solutions. In just a second, we'll see how successful the company has been in monetizing this but at least it is clear from the above infographic that they haven't been slow to adapt to a changing landscape. As you can see, they have a strong ecosystem of agents including Workday Agents and so it seems that the company has been quick to react to the boom in agentic AI.

Growth Accelerated

Workday reported their FY2026 Q4 earnings in late February. Let's start with the top line numbers. The company generated total revenues of $2.532 billion, up 14.5% YoY. This is an acceleration of growth from the previous quarter's 12.6% increase and so it actually seems that Workday's business trajectory is improving. For their subscription revenues specifically, there was also growth acceleration to 15.7%.

In the year ago quarter, company-wide growth was at 15% and so really the company has shown great resilience. While this shows that AI hasn't created strong tailwinds, it also shows that AI hasn't caused apocalyptic results like many had feared. Workday also managed to beat top line expectations by $8.81 million and so the fourth quarter was one of outperformance.

Lastly, it should be mentioned here that Workday does have quite a respectable gross revenue retention. Currently, this metric is at 97% and so that shows most clients decide to continue using their services. While an even higher level in retention is always desired, I would say that the current position is enough to suggest that Workday is offering a decent value proposition to customers and that is making them stay for the most part.

Margin Expands

Another sign that the apocalypse hasn't materialized so far is the fact that margin actually expanded by a healthy amount in Q4. Workday reported a non-GAAP operating margin of 30.6%, up 413 basis points from the prior year period's 26.4%. While this obviously shows strong cost and/or expense control, it also shows that the company's pricing power hasn't been eroded significantly. If Workday's solutions were truly getting disrupted by AI, their pricing power would likely evaporate and so the fact that margin has held up quite well suggests that fears are overblown.

With operating margin being healthy, the bottom line was also robust for Q4. As shown above, they reported an adjusted EPS of $2.47, up 28.65% YoY. This is a nice acceleration from the previous quarter and also represents a beat on the bottom line. We'll get into their guidance next, but consensus expectations for EPS do seem a little concerning as we head into their FY2027.

Guidance Looks Respectable

Firstly, Workday's subscription revenue guidance for Q1 was below expectations and so there may be some disappointment there. However, let's take a look at their full year estimates to see where things are going on a longer timeframe.

For total revenue growth, they are projecting between 11% and 12% for FY2027. That would be a notable slowdown from FY2026's 13.1% increase. Why that is definitely not favorable, I would say that this deceleration is far from what would be classified as apocalyptic. For subscription revenues, a moderate slowdown is also expected from 14.5% to the range of 12% to 13% and so again, nothing catastrophic.

As for margin, Workday sees non-GAAP operating margin expansion of 44 basis points. While this isn't nearly as impressive as FY2026's 368 basis point improvement, there is likely to be expansion nonetheless. Therefore, core profitability of the company is expected to stay resilient. As mentioned earlier, major business disruption rarely results in margin expansion and so there are further signals that Workday is in fact holding its ground rather nicely.

On their earnings call, CFO Zane Rowe stated that this outlook takes into account "an accelerated pace of AI investment across both product and go-to-market." He went on to say that "we remain focused on both GAAP and non-GAAP margin expansion, albeit at a slower pace in the near term than what we previously communicated." Analysts also highlighted short-term margin risk post earnings. In my view, while this may be a risk, this lower pace of margin expansion is very much forgivable if you're a long term investor. I believe the company's commitment to AI is in fact a bullish sign as it shows that management is focused on the future rather than short-termism.

AI Push Is Clear

If Workday's enterprise solutions were truly getting disrupted, adoption would drop sharply. Instead, there are signs that businesses continue to choose Workday. In early to mid March, a press release showed that Fairview Health Services had selected Workday "to modernize HR, finance, and supply chain on One AIPowered Platform." With Fairview being a very large organization with 34,000 employees, this sends a signal that Workday remains highly relevant today.

In addition to continued adoption, innovation is also occurring at Workday. In mid to late March, the company introduced Sana. This is actually a portfolio of AI solutions. You can read more about this in their press release but the launch of Sana for Workday, Sana Self-Service Agent, and Sana Enterprise clearly show that they are on top of their AI game. While investors fear AI disruption, the company itself seems to be going all-in with regards to conversational AI, agentic AI, and enterprise AI.

Valuation At Depressed Levels

As you can see in the chart above, Workday's forward P/E ratio really hasn't had time to catch a break recently. The multiple is now just a hair above multiyear lows and at the current reading of 12.45, I would say that Workday's risk/reward is heavily in the bulls' favor.

Today, it was shown that Workday's fundamentals have been largely resilient. Growth actually accelerated from Q3 and margin also expanded. Moving forward, guidance isn't perfect by any means. However, the estimates also clearly show that an apocalyptic event isn't coming anytime soon. The main risk moving forward is slower margin expansion. As demonstrated by the consensus EPS growth estimates for FY2027, bottom line growth may be weaker than what we have seen and so that could be a risk to near term stock performance. However, for long term investors, I view the AI investments as crucial in order to secure the future of the business. I personally wouldn't mind some short term pain for long term gain.

At current levels, the forward P/E seems to imply that the market thinks the end may be coming for Workday. I believe this conclusion would be laughable. Looking forward, growth is expected to continue being relatively resilient and the AI push discussed above shows that they are very much relevant in this day and age of AI.

Conclusion

In my view, Workday stock has been unjustifiably punished in the past few months. With declines in excess of 40%, I believe the setup is now very favorable for long term investors. This is a classic example of being greedy when others are fearful. SaaSpocalypse fears were shown to be irrational in today's analysis and the real risk is just some slower margin expansion over the near term due to AI investments. The fundamentals and outlook in no rational way justify a valuation that's this severely depressed. As a result, I'm initiating Workday at a buy rating.

data.title: Lumen: Look At The Balance Sheet To Get A Feel For Future Growth
data.description:
If I had to pick one stock that I have failed to crack, my first pick would be Lumen Technologies, Inc. (LUMN). Almost 7 years ago, I made a small profit with LUMN, but since then, the going has been difficult. I was bearish on Lumen when I should have been bullish and vice versa.

Last October, after a couple of years sitting on the sidelines, I turned bullish on the prospects for Lumen as I thought the company was on the right track to grow adjusted EBITDA in 2026. Since then, the stock has lost just over 1% of its value. Last January, I made a case for why Lumen deserves to trade at premium valuation multiples compared to legacy telcos. My argument was that Lumen was no longer a legacy telco business but a tech business. LUMN has lost ~15% since then.

Now that Lumen has reported Q4 2025 earnings and set a baseline for 2026 expectations, I wanted to revisit my thesis. Rather than looking at all aspects of my thesis, I thought it best to look at the timeline for PCF contract revenue conversion cycles to get a better understanding of when Lumen will finally get a revenue boost from the secured PCF contracts.

Understanding When PCF Contracts Hit Revenue

Lumen is solving real-world connectivity problems for hyperscalers who are aggressively investing billions of dollars in AI infrastructure. This is where PCF contracts are used. Internet bandwidth through traditional networks has been proven insufficient to handle the data loads required to train LLM models. A PCF contract allows a customer to get access to a private, custom fiber network with a very high capacity and low latency. Some of Lumen's major PCF customers include tech giants such as Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Alphabet Inc. (GOOG), and Anthropic (the company behind the widely popular Claude LLM).

As highlighted by the management in the Q4 earnings release, Lumen has so far signed PCF contracts worth $13 billion.

Exhibit 1: Lumen's 2025 financial highlights

Despite stellar growth in PCF contracts, Lumen has not seen a material impact from them on its revenue growth. This is because of how these contracts are packaged. These are often long-term contracts where Lumen has agreed to deploy infrastructure over several years. What this means is that Lumen does not get to report the total contract value as revenue in the current period. Going by the management guidance, we should expect these contracts to contribute at least $500 million to revenue annually before 2030. This could happen as soon as 2028 as well. For at least another 2 years, we are unlikely to see a meaningful impact on Lumen's revenue growth resulting from the signed PCF deals.

Although that may be discouraging, the good news is that Lumen's balance sheet is mostly immune to the capital expenditure requirements arising from the expected buildout of these networks. This is because Lumen collects upfront cash payments from customers who sign PCF contracts. This characteristic of PCF contracts has helped Lumen a great deal to keep its liquidity profile stable in the short run.

A quick look at Lumen's operating cash flow movements and deferred revenue confirms how PCF contracts are currently accounted for. The company's total deferred revenue has ballooned from $2.68 billion in Q1 2024 to $7.41 billion in Q4 2025. Deferred revenue has started accelerating from the fourth quarter of 2024, when Lumen went all-in on PCF contracts. A breakdown of this deferred revenue reveals that Lumen investors are in for a long wait. According to company filings, $6.4 billion of this deferred revenue is classified as long-term deferred revenue, meaning that only $1 billion in deferred revenue is expected to be realized in the next 12 months.

There are clear signs that Lumen has already started the buildout for these PCF contracts. CapEx grew from around $1 billion in Q3 2025 to $1.64 billion in Q4, coinciding with the start of Lumen's aggressive investments in PCF buildouts.

Exhibit 2: Capital expenditures

The absolute CapEx run rate will decline in 2026 because of the recently completed AT&T deal. The resulting CapEx will almost entirely focus on new growth avenues, primarily building the necessary infrastructure to move PCF contract revenue from the balance sheet to the income statement. For now, Lumen is dealing with a lot of challenges while paving the way for an influx of new, high-quality revenue starting in a few years. Lumen's large enterprise segment revenue only increased by $5 million sequentially in Q4, in complete contrast to how PCF contract backlog has grown. Lumen is clearly suffering from a timing issue here. Interestingly, I believe the market is still valuing Lumen based on its continued YoY revenue declines, when in reality, the company has built a strong foundation to reverse the course in the future.

The Current Valuation Fails To Accurately Account For The PCF Backlog

Lumen has seen its revenue erode in the past few years. Profitability has been a distant reality. And the market is valuing Lumen as a legacy telecom business that is about to die. This premise is wrong, in my opinion.

PCF revenues are likely to enjoy much higher EBITDA margins than the dying, legacy business of Lumen. PCF customers are injecting upfront cash to fund the CapEx required for buildouts, so when Lumen reports revenue, margins will be high. In addition to this, the PCF business is likely to have structurally lower maintenance costs compared to the legacy lease business. I don't believe Mr. Market has fully grasped the fact that deferred revenue, which is currently reported as a liability, represents a multi-billion-dollar high-margin revenue stream.

Today, Lumen is valued at a forward EV/EBITDA multiple of 7.9x.

Going by the management guidance, if Lumen were to generate $550 million in ARR from PCF contracts starting in 2028, we are looking at new recurring EBITDA of around $358 million (EBITDA margin of 65% on new PCF revenue).

Here's where the multiple expansion will happen.

Legacy telcos generally trade for around 6x EV/EBITDA. However, digital infrastructure solutions providers typically trade at much higher multiples that sometimes stretch as high as 15x EV/EBITDA. Given Lumen's strong moat within the PCF sector, I believe applying an EV/EBITDA multiple of 12x makes sense. Going by this, the PCF business will be valued at around $4.3 billion.

In simpler terms, I believe the PCF segment itself is worth over $4 billion. Lumen's equity is currently valued at $7.2 billion in the market. I expect the blended EV/EBITDA multiple to expand meaningfully through 2030 as the contribution from the PCF business continues to grow.

Risks

Lumen is still in a business transformation phase. A lot can still go wrong. I am keeping an eye on how the company handles aggressive investments while keeping its balance sheet health in check. Failure to do this will almost certainly lead to a market rout as investors, including myself, still have flashbacks of Lumen's struggles.

Then there is the continued drag on profitability and growth stemming from the dying legacy business. Since this business is eroding much faster than the new PCF and AI businesses, the short-run financial performance is going to look ugly. Amid macro and geopolitical tensions, this could lead to a prolonged decline in the stock price before an eventual recovery. For this reason, I believe LUMN is only suitable for investors with a strong stomach.

Investors cannot and should not forget the debt burden too. Although the balance sheet position is much better than it was a few years ago, post 2027, debt maturities will accelerate.

Takeaway

Lumen is attractively valued. Growth may seem lackluster for now, but a good look at the balance sheet suggests Lumen is positioning itself to benefit from a revenue windfall from PCF contracts in a few years. This is not accurately baked into Lumen's market value today, in my opinion. Although a few risks need to be closely monitored, I believe Lumen is very attractive today.
